content.full_text:
Farn PolrrIcll PnncucBs CouvttssloN
P.O. Box 807 . ,l2lt J Street . Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-08u6
August 21,2000
Greg Zerovnik
Director of Marketing
Kawama.com
201F. Sandpointe
Santa Ana, Califomia 92707
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-00-135
Dear Mr. Zerovnik:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the gift limitation and
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l The Act requires the
Commission to provide written advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question
or to that person's authorized representative. (Section 831la(b); Regulation 18329(bxl),
enclosed.) However, the Commission does not respond to requests where all the material facts
have not been provided. (Regulation 18329(bX8XC).) Since your request does not include
material facts needed by us to fully assess your situation, we are unable to provide you with
formal written advice.2 However, in order to provide you with some guidance, we have included
additional information on the applicable law. Please bear in mind that this letter is based on the
facts you have presented to us. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
Is a procurement supervisor employed by a California school district subject to
provisions of the Act?
' Gou"**rrt Code sections 8 1000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at tttle 2, sections 18 i 09 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
' Pleur" note that only formal written advice confers the immunity provided under Section 83114(b) and
Regulation 18329(b)(7). Informal assistance does not provide immunity. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)
1


File No. I-00-135
Page2
If the winner of a lottery co-sponsored by your company, Kawama.com
("Kawama"), were subject to the Act, would he or she be prohibited from
receiving the winnings?
3. 
Is such a winner required to report his or her winnings?
May a winner who is subject to the Act participate in a governmental decision
involving whether Kawama's services will be utilized?
CONCLUSIONS
A procurement supervisor employed by a Califomia schqol district is
subject to the Act's provisions if he or she is a "public official" as defined
by the Act.
If the winner were an elected officer, candidate for elective office, or
designated employee as provided by Section 89503, then he or she would
be subject to the Act's gift limitation and may be prohibited from
receiving the winnings over $300.
A winner subject to the gift limitation will also be subject to the Act's
reporting requirements. If the school administrator or educator is a
designated employee, he or she must review the conflict of interest code of
his or her agency to see which sources of gifts are reportable.
4. 
We are unable to make a determination with regard to this question.
FACTS
Based on your correspondence, your company, Kawama, offers online procurement
solutions to schools and districts at no charge. If a school or district uses your online
procurement methods to make a purchase, you receive compensation from the providing vendor
or supplier. This compensation can be characterized as a transaction fee. At no time does your
company take title or create a monetary exchange relationship between the vendor company and
a participating school or district.
In an effort to publicize your company and products to schools, you attend various trade
shows and conferences. Attendees to such conferences typically include avaiety of school
administrators and educators, the mix of which varies depending on the sponsoring organization.
2
4
1
2.
3


File No. I-00-135
Page 3
Kawama plans to maintain a booth at an Association of School Business Officers, International
("ASBO") conference to be held in Minnesota in September. You anticipate that some attendees
at this conference will be school administrators and educators from California.
One way of drawing foot traffic to your booth at these conferences is a legal lottery to
give away a two-year lease on a car. The presumed fair market value of the car lease is about
$11,000. According to your company's Trade Show Coordinator, Sandy Frye, Kawama itself
will not give away the lease. Instead, Kawama will provide money to a company, Traffic
Builders, which provides services designed to increase foot haffic to conference booths. Traffic
Builders will facilitate the purchase of the lease and handle all matters related to the cax's lease,
insurance, licensing, etc. However, the lease itself will be given to the winner in the name of
Kawama and ASBO. Promotional materials about the lottery would state that ASBO is
conducting the giveaway and that Kawama and ASBO are "co-sponsors."
Only conference attendees will take part in the lottery. To obtain a lottery ticket, a
conference attendee would have to visit your booth and participate in a virhral reality car race
computer game. Regardless of the attendee's game score, the attendee would "eam" a ticket for
the drawing. You have been assured by ASBO's show organizers that the lottery, per se, is legal
in Minnesota.
AI\ALYSIS
Based on the facts which you have provided, it is possible that your company may
become subject to provisions of the Acd, although we cannot make such a determination at this
time. We, therefore, are providing you the following general information:
1. Gift Limitation
The Act provides that no elected state officer, elected officer of a local government
agency, or other individual specified in Section 872004 of the Act shall accept gifts from any
3 See Section 8g52l,which provides that any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or
expenditure in violation of this chapter is liable in a civil action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to
three times the amount of the unlawful honorariurn, gift, or expenditure.
a Section 87200 identifies elected state officers, judges and commissioners of courts of the judicial branch
of government, members of the Public Utilities Commission, members of the State Energy Resources Conservation
and Development Commission, members of the Fair Political Practices Commission, members of the California
Coastal Commission, members of planning commissions, members of the board of supervisors, district attorneys,
county counsels, county fieasurers, and chief administative officers of counties, rnayors, city managers, city
attorneys, city teasurers, chief administrative officers and members of city councils of cities, and other public
officials who manage public investments, and candidates for any of these offices at any election.


File No. I-00-135
Page 4
single source in any calendar year with a total value of more than $300 dollars.s
(Section 89503(a).) This same prohibition also applies to candidates for elective state office, for
judicial office, or for elective office in a local govemment agency. (Section 89503(b).)
In addition, the Act prohibits any member of a state board or commission or designated
employee of a state or local government agency from accepting gifts from any single source in
any calendar year with a total value of more than $300 if the employee would be required to
report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic
interests. (Section 89503(c).)
The gift limitation does not apply to a person in his or her capacity as a part-time member
of the governing board of any public institution of higher education unless that position is an
elective office. (Section 89503(d).)
The Act defines a "designated employee" as any officer, employee, member, or
consultant of any agency whose position with the agency:
(a) Is exempt from the state civil service system by virtue of
subdivision (a), (c), (d), (e), (0, (g), or (m) of Section 4 of Article
VII of the Constitution, unless the position is elective or solely
secretarial, clerical, or manual;
(b) Is elective, other than an elective state officer;
(c) Is designated in a Conflict of Interest Code because the position entails the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a
material effect on any financial interest;
(d) Is involved as a state employee at other than a clerical or
ministerial level in the functions of negotiating or signing any
contract awarded through competitive bidding, in making decisions
in conjunction with the competitive bidding process, or in
negotiating, signing, or making decisions on contracts executed
pursuant to Section 10122 of the Public Contract Code.
(Section 82019.)
Consequently, if a school administrator (e.g., a procurement supervisor) or educator
attending the ASBO conference is an elected officer, candidate for elective office, or meets any
of the criteria for being a designated employee, then that individual is subject to the Act's gift
limitation.
5 The gift limit amount is adjusted biennially by the Commission to reflect changes in the Consumer Price
Index. As of January 1,1999, the amount is $300. (Section 89503(0; Regulation 189a0.2(a).)


File No. I-00-135
Page 5
For purposes of the Act, a "gift" is any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.
(Section 82028(a).) However, Regulation 18946.5 provides that an award received by a public
official in a bona fide compqtition, unrelated to one's status as a public official, will be heated as
income under the Act, rather than as a gift. Thus, awards qualiffing under Regulation 18946.5
are not subject to the Act's restrictions on gifts to public officials.
Based on the facts you provided, your lottery does not qualiff as a "bona fide
competition" within the meaning of this regulation. We have previously advised that if raffle
ticket holders were primarily officials or candidates regulated by the Act, as in a raffle held at a
conference of public officials, the raffle would be related to official status, and therefore not
subject to the exception of Regulation 18946.5. (NemeroffAdvice Letter,No. A-99-148;
Allen Advice Letter, No. A-95-148.) Similarly, your lottery will be related to official status since
participation in your lottery will not be open to the public and since the primary attendees at the
ASBO conference will be school administrators and educators. As such, if a public official wins
your lottery, he or she may not receive the car lease, valued at $11,000, if receipt of a gift in this
amount will violate the $300 gift limitation.
Nevertheless, a gift from a donor is neither accepted nor received if it is returned,
donated, or reimbursed in any manner set forth below:
(1) The gift is returned to the donor, or to the donor's agent or
intermediary, unused, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance. In
such event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to
disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity expense; or
(2) The gift is donated, unused, to a charitable organization
within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, without being claimed as a
charitable contribution for tax purposes. In such event, neither the
recipient nor the donor shall be required to disclose the receipt or
making of a gift or activity expense; or
(3) The gift is donated, unused, to a state, local, or federal
government agency, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance,
without being claimed as a deduction for tax purposes. In such
event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to
disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity expense; or
(a) The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance,
reimburses the donor, or the donor's agent or intermediary, for all
or a portion of the gift. In such event, the value of the gift is
reduced by the amount of the reimbursement, and the amount of
any gift or activity expense which must be disclosed is reduced by
the amount of the reimbursement. (Regulation 189a3(a).)


File No. I-00-135
Page 6
Therefore, any of the actions detailed in Regulation 18943(a) may be taken with respect
to winnings of your lottery.6
2. Reporting Requirements
The Act requires specified officials, candidates and designated employees to file a Form
700, statement of economic interests ("SEI"), each year disclosing certain investments, real
property interests, income, and gifts that they have received during the year. (Section 87200
et seq.) According to these reporting rules, even if a school administrator or educator were to
receive a gift with a value of $300 or less, he or she may be required to report any source of gifts
aggregating $50 or more on his or her SEI. (Section 87207(a).)
3. Conflict of Interest Rules
The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from
bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
You have asked whether a school administrator or educator who may attend the ASBO
conference and win the car lease in your lottery may subsequently participate in a governmental
decision involving whether Kawama's services will be utilized. We are unable to answer this
question since the Commission does not respond to requests where the requestor is a third parry
seeking advice about another person's duties under the ActT and where all the material facts have
not been provided. (Regulations 18329(b)(8)(B) and (C).) We are, however, enclosing the
Commission document entitled "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview" to provide you
with some additional guidance. We hope this information will be useful to you.
u Pl"ur" note that the Commission only provides advice relating to the Act. The Commission does not
provide advice regarding other state or federal laws or policies which might limit a California ASBO conference
attendee from legally accepting the lottery winnings, nor does the Commission advise as to whether the lottery itself
complies with other laws.
' Th" Co--ission will respond to requests from a "third party" only if the requestor has been authorized
by the person with duties under the Act. (Regulation 18329(bX8XB).)


File No. I-00-135
Page 7
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(et6) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
0Cd-illt\+,\*
By
atalie
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:NB
Enclosures

May a winner who is subject to the Act participate in a governmental decision
involving whether Kawama's services will be utilized?

designated employee as provided by Section 89503, then he or she would

receiving the winnings over $300.
A winner subject to the gift limitation will also be subject to the Act's
reporting requirements. If the school administrator or educator is a
designated employee, he or she must review the conflict of interest code of
his or her agency to see which sources of gifts are reportable.
4. 
We are unable to make a determination with regard to this question.

become subject to provisions of the Acd, although we cannot make such a determination at this
time. We, therefore, are providing you the following general information:
1. Gift Limitation
The Act provides that no elected state officer, elected officer of a local government

3 See Section 8g52l,which provides that any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or
expenditure in violation of this chapter is liable in a civil action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to
three times the amount of the unlawful honorariurn, gift, or expenditure.

of government, members of the Public Utilities Commission, members of the State Energy Resources Conservation
and Development Commission, members of the Fair Political Practices Commission, members of the California
Coastal Commission, members of planning commissions, members of the board of supervisors, district attorneys,

attorneys, city teasurers, chief administrative officers and members of city councils of cities, and other public
officials who manage public investments, and candidates for any of these offices at any election.
single source in any calendar year with a total value of more than $300 dollars.s
(Section 89503(a).) This same prohibition also applies to candidates for elective state office, for
judicial office, or for elective office in a local govemment agency. (Section 89503(b).)
In addition, the Act prohibits any member of a state board or commission or designated

interests. (Section 89503(c).)

elective office. (Section 89503(d).)
The Act defines a "designated employee" as any officer, employee, member, or
consultant of any agency whose position with the agency:
(a) Is exempt from the state civil service system by virtue of
subdivision (a), (c), (d), (e), (0, (g), or (m) of Section 4 of Article
VII of the Constitution, unless the position is elective or solely
secretarial, clerical, or manual;
(b) Is elective, other than an elective state officer;
(c) Is designated in a Conflict of Interest Code because the position entails the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a
material effect on any financial interest;
(d) Is involved as a state employee at other than a clerical or
ministerial level in the functions of negotiating or signing any
contract awarded through competitive bidding, in making decisions
in conjunction with the competitive bidding process, or in
negotiating, signing, or making decisions on contracts executed
pursuant to Section 10122 of the Public Contract Code.
(Section 82019.)

attending the ASBO conference is an elected officer, candidate for elective office, or meets any
of the criteria for being a designated employee, then that individual is subject to the Act's gift

Index. As of January 1,1999, the amount is $300. (Section 89503(0; Regulation 189a0.2(a).)
For purposes of the Act, a "gift" is any payment that confers a personal benefit on the

are not subject to the Act's restrictions on gifts to public officials.

participation in your lottery will not be open to the public and since the primary attendees at the

Nevertheless, a gift from a donor is neither accepted nor received if it is returned,
donated, or reimbursed in any manner set forth below:
(1) The gift is returned to the donor, or to the donor's agent or
intermediary, unused, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance. In
such event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to
disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity expense; or

within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, without being claimed as a
charitable contribution for tax purposes. In such event, neither the
recipient nor the donor shall be required to disclose the receipt or
making of a gift or activity expense; or
(3) The gift is donated, unused, to a state, local, or federal
government agency, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance,
without being claimed as a deduction for tax purposes. In such
event, neither the recipient nor the donor shall be required to
disclose the receipt or making of a gift or activity expense; or
(a) The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance,
reimburses the donor, or the donor's agent or intermediary, for all
or a portion of the gift. In such event, the value of the gift is
reduced by the amount of the reimbursement, and the amount of
any gift or activity expense which must be disclosed is reduced by
the amount of the reimbursement. (Regulation 189a3(a).)
Therefore, any of the actions detailed in Regulation 18943(a) may be taken with respect
to winnings of your lottery.6

The Act requires specified officials, candidates and designated employees to file a Form
700, statement of economic interests ("SEI"), each year disclosing certain investments, real
property interests, income, and gifts that they have received during the year. (Section 87200
et seq.) According to these reporting rules, even if a school administrator or educator were to
receive a gift with a value of $300 or less, he or she may be required to report any source of gifts

officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from

official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial

You have asked whether a school administrator or educator who may attend the ASBO
conference and win the car lease in your lottery may subsequently participate in a governmental
decision involving whether Kawama's services will be utilized. We are unable to answer this

with some additional guidance. We hope this information will be useful to you.
